Patch Set 12: Code-Review-1

Lustre readahead is incompatible with kernel readahead framework because:
1. Lustre tries to issue optimized RPC by trimming the readaehad window from kernel, which poses a problem of huge penalty because kernel seems to not smart enough to detect this case;
2. It seems hard to implement stride readahead from current kernel framework. When the stride exceeds backing_dev_info::ra_pages, kernel will think it of random read.

I would suggest we land the first two patches in this series because this one will definitely need more work.

Patch Set 36:

btw, ll_readpages() carries the number of read ahead pages suggested by kernel, and Lustre should trim it(instead of extending it) by the boundary of RPC and lock extent. We should honor the request size by kernel otherwise it won't work well.

If all the work of readahead were to bring in as many pages as it could be, it would be too easy.

Patch Set 36:

I agree that we need two set of algorithm, but only one should be used at any time. If the read pattern is detected as stride read, we should disable the kernel readahead, or vice versa.

> We already have ra_max_pages to limit the total number of outstanding pages consumed by 
> readahead, this should be used to avoid consuming too much memory.

This is not used any more in the new algorithm. However, I don't think there will be a problem because kernel will adjust how many pages to read ahead by the current status of memory pressure. This is exactly why I don't think the current implementation would work because the suggested req_size by kernel seems to be ignored.

Patch Set 37:

(1 comment)

One other *enormous* benefit from this patch:
It appears to fix whatever it is that breaks in strided readahead when we read a file from multiple threads on the same node.

For example, without this patch:
Reading a file 47K at a time (on some slow-ish VMs) from one thread:
mpirun --allow-run-as-root -n 1 /shared/testcases/IOR_DIR/IOR -o /mnt/cent7s02/ior/iorfile -r -k -E -t 47K -b 47K -s 80000
read          786.22     786.22      786.22      0.00       0.21       0.21        0.21      0.00   4.67028   EXCEL

From two threads: (same line except -n = 2 and -s 4000):
read      92.98      47.00      47.00      0.001136   39.48      0.008979   39.49      0

~80% performance drop. This is typical...

With this patch, one thread:
read      835.62     47.00      47.00      0.000711   4.39       0.000501   4.39       0

Two threads:
read      829.68     47.00      47.00      0.001756   4.42       0.004775   4.43       0

~830 MB/s is close to the limit for real reads on this little VM system.  I expect a real system would show large benefits going to multiple threads, though I would have to verify.

Anyway, this is a huge benefit, and note that the I/O pattern is strided (at least, in the multi-thread case).  Still trying to find time to do a complete review.

Patch Set 43:

> (1 comment)

Of course we don't expect the LRU size to be empty. It's all about the turnover rate. OSC tries its best to evict some pages to free more LRU slots when the available LRU slot is in shortage. However, if applications generate I/O faster than the evicting speed, or for some reason the OSC can't evict pages(for example, most of pages are being locked). The application will stall and poor performance is expected. Essentially enough LRU space is a necessity for performance.

I don't agree that readahead should evict pages by itself. Readahead is supposed to be heuristic and it doesn't have enough information to choose which pages should be evicted.

Patch Set 45:

What I mean is not that we should expect it to be empty, it's that on a busy node, we should expect it to be mostly full.  We shouldn't dramatically change our reading behavior because the LRU is mostly full.  We're doing readahead because we think there's a very high chance those pages will be needed, so that should be enough reason to evict older things from cache.

I agree we might want to make readahead less aggressive when the LRU is near full.

How about this:
If readahead doesn't evict pages, how should reading evict pages?  Only when we explicitly read in new pages?  If readahead never evicts pages, that effectively means we disable readahead for files larger than the LRU.  That can't be right, can it?

Patch Set 44:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 44:

Hm, reading your note again Jinshan, I wonder if we're talking past each other.  I think readahead should stay turned on when we're low on LRU slots, because reading a file larger than cache or when the cache is mostly full is a normal and important thing.  The cache being mostly full (or completely full if the node is busy) is normal and we shouldn't turn off readahead there.

That's all I mean.  There may be a case for making readahead less aggressive when the LRU space is low or out, but I don't think we should turn it off.

Thanks for clarifying about the LRU.  I had forgotten that it tries to keep some free space.  Still, a streaming read can fill that space quickly, and reading when the cache is mostly full is important.

commit.subject: LU-8964 llite: Make readahead async
commit.message:
LU-8964 llite: Make readahead async

Removed current Lustre implementation of readahead and replaced it
with in-kernel implementation. Adopted code for in-kernel readahead
usage. Perform readahead requests asynchronously from current I/O.

In combination with parallelization of generic I/O this patch allows
significantly improve read performance.

Serial read with fake RW on server side:
master (2.10.54_13):
20971520000 bytes (21 GB) copied, 9.99935 s, 2.1 GB/s
this patch (2.10.54_14):
20971520000 bytes (21 GB) copied, 7.82126 s, 2.7 GB/s

Stride read with fake RW on server side:           |   master    | this patch
reads <opts> -n 64                                 | 2.10.54_13  | 2.10.54_14
---------------------------------------------------+-------------+------------
reads -l 256 -o 128 -s 134217728 -b 1048576 -a 128 | 318.175MB/s | 649.091MB/s
reads -l 128 -o 64 -s 134217728 -b 1048576 -a 64   | 129.744MB/s | 857.413MB/s
reads -l 64 -o 32 -s 134217728 -b 1048576 -a 32    | 149.868MB/s | 948.817MB/s
reads -l 32 -o 16 -s 134217728 -b 1048576 -a 16    | 171.335MB/s | 785.450MB/s
reads -l 16 -o 8 -s 134217728 -b 1048576 -a 8      | 165.078MB/s | 918.306MB/s
reads -l 8 -o 4 -s 134217728 -b 1048576 -a 4       | 204.870MB/s | 840.595MB/s
reads -l 4 -o 2 -s 134217728 -b 1048576 -a 2       | 459.216MB/s | 1118.61MB/s
reads -l 2 -o 1 -s 134217728 -b 1048576 -a 1       | 486.409MB/s | 1002.01MB/s
